summary: The Docker-based reproduction clones the audited Caddy snapshot and confirms that a remote admin client certificate scoped to one config subtree can fetch a sibling config object that only shares the same path prefix.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should make an mTLS-authenticated remote admin GET to /config/apps/http/servers/tenant-admin succeed even though the client certificate is only authorized for /config/apps/http/servers/tenant-a, and the response should contain the unauthorized sibling object.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh cloned commit db256b53e517191f52eca4ea11d415d7a11e729f, built the reproduction image, and ran TestEvoHuntRemoteAdminPathPrefixBypass successfully. The real admin handler processed the mTLS request, and the injected test confirmed HTTP 200 plus the sibling tenant-admin response object.
verification_method: verify.sh copies the pinned repository into a writable workspace, injects remote_admin_prefix_bypass_test.go, runs go test . -run '^TestEvoHuntRemoteAdminPathPrefixBypass$' -count=1 -v, and relies on the test assertions plus a log grep for "verified_chains":1 to distinguish the unauthorized sibling read from unrelated handler behavior.
evidence_produced_by_run: The test run logged "verified_chains":1 for the remote admin request, showing that the request traversed the mTLS-protected remote admin path.
TestEvoHuntRemoteAdminPathPrefixBypass passed only after asserting that the response code was 200 and that the body contained the sibling tenant-admin object with :81.
The run completed with [evohunt] SUCCESS: remote admin ACL prefix bypass reproduced.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit or ref, and mounts that checkout into the container.
